text:
- ''That will do,' said he; 'I could not ask for anything better. Capital! capital!' He seemed quite enthusiastic and rubbed his hands together in the most genial fashion. He was such a comfortable-looking man that it was quite a pleasure to look at him.
- ' 'You are looking for a situation, miss?' he asked.
- ' 'Yes, sir.'
- ' 'As governess?'
- ' 'Yes, sir.'
- ' 'And what salary do you ask?'
- ' 'I had £ 4 a month in my last place with Colonel Spence Munro.'
- ' 'Oh, tut, tut! sweating-rank sweating!' he cried, throwing his fat hands out into the air like a man who is in a boiling passion. 'How could anyone offer so pitiful a sum to a lady with such attractions and accomplishments?'
- ' 'My accomplishments, sir, may be less than you imagine,' said I. 'A little French, a little German, music, and drawing-'
- ''Tut, tut!' he cried. 'This is all quite beside the question. The point is, have you or have you not the bearing and deportment of a lady? There it is in a nutshell. If you have not, you are not fitted for the rearing of a child who may some day play a considerable part in the history of the country. But if you have why, then, how could any gentleman ask you to condescend to accept anything under the three figures? Your salary with me, madam, would commence at £ 100 a year.'  
'You may imagine, Mr. Holmes, that to me, destitute as I was, such an offer seemed almost too good to be true. The gentleman, however, seeing perhaps the look of incredulity upon my face, opened a pocketbook and took out a note.  
''It is also my custom,' said he, smiling in the most pleasant fashion until his eyes were just two little shining slits amid the white creases of his face, 'to advance to my young ladies half their salary beforehand, so that they may meet any little expenses of their journey and their wardrobe.'  
'It seemed to me that I had never met so fascinating and so thoughtful a man. As I was already in debt to my tradesmen, the advance was a great convenience, and yet there was something unnatural about the whole transaction which made me wish to know a little more before I quite committed myself.  
- ' 'May I ask where you live, sir?' said I.
- ' 'Hampshire. Charming rural place. The Copper Beeches, five miles on the far side of Winchester. It is  
the most lovely country, my dear young lady, and the dearest old country-house.'  
- ''And my duties, sir? I should be glad to know what they would be.'
- ' 'One child-one dear little romper just six years old. Oh, if you could see him killing cockroaches with a slipper! Smack! smack! smack! Three gone before you could wink!' He leaned back in his chair and laughed his eyes into his head again.
- 'I was a little startled at the nature of the child's amusement, but the father's laughter made me think that perhaps he was joking.
- ' 'My sole duties, then,' I asked, 'are to take charge of a single child?'
- ' 'No, no, not the sole, not the sole, my dear young lady,' he cried. 'Your duty would be, as I am sure your good sense would suggest, to obey any little commands my wife might give, provided always that they were such commands as a lady might with propriety obey. You see no difficulty, heh?'
- ' 'I should be happy to make myself useful.'
- ''Quite so. In dress now, for example. We are faddy people, you know-faddy but kind-hearted. If you were asked to wear any dress which we might give you, you would not object to our little whim. Heh?'
- ''No,' said I, considerably astonished at his words.
- ''Or to sit here, or sit there, that would not be offensive to you?'
- ' 'Oh, no.'
- ' 'Or to cut your hair quite short before you come to us?'  
'I could hardly believe my ears. As you may observe, Mr. Holmes, my hair is somewhat luxuriant, and of a rather peculiar tint of chestnut. It has been considered artistic. I could not dream of sacrificing it in this offhand fashion.  
''I am afraid that that is quite impossible,' said I. He had been watching me eagerly out of his small eyes, and I could see a shadow pass over his face as I spoke.  
- ' 'I am afraid that it is quite essential,' said he. 'It is a little fancy of my wife's, and ladies' fancies, you know, madam, ladies' fancies must be consulted. And so you won't cut your hair?'
- ' 'No, sir, I really could not,' I answered firmly.
- ' 'Ah, very well; then that quite settles the matter. It is a pity, because in other respects you would really  
have done very nicely. In that case, Miss Stoper, I had best inspect a few more of your young ladies.'  
'The manageress had sat all this while busy with her papers without a word to either of us, but she glanced at me now with so much annoyance upon her face that I could not help suspecting that she had lost a handsome commission through my refusal.  
- ''Do you desire your name to be kept upon the books?' she asked.
- ' 'If you please, Miss Stoper.'
- ''Well, really, it seems rather useless, since you refuse the most excellent offers in this fashion,' said she sharply. 'You can hardly expect us to exert ourselves to find another such opening for you. Goodday to you, Miss Hunter.' She struck a gong upon the table, and I was shown out by the page.  
'Well, Mr. Holmes, when I got back to my lodgings and found little enough in the cupboard, and two or three bills upon the table. I began to ask myself whether I had not done a very foolish thing. After all, if these people had strange fads and expected obedience on the most extraordinary matters, they were at least ready to pay for their eccentricity. Very few governesses in England are getting £ 100 a year. Besides, what use was my hair to me? Many people are improved by wearing it short and perhaps I should be among the number. Next day I was inclined to think that I had made a mistake, and by the day after I was sure of it. I had almost overcome my pride so far as to go back to the agency and inquire whether the place was still open when I received this letter from the gentleman himself. I have it here and I will read it to you:  
- ' 'The Copper Beeches, near Winchester. ' 'D ear M iss H unter :
- ' 'Miss Stoper has very kindly given me your address, and I write from here to ask you whether you have reconsidered your decision. My wife is very anxious that you should come, for she has been much attracted by my description of you. We are willing to give £ 30 a quarter, or £ 120 a year, so as to recompense you for any little inconvenience which our fads may cause you. They are not very exacting, after all. My wife is fond of a particular shade of electric blue and would like you to wear such a dress indoors in the morning. You need not, however, go to the expense of purchasing one, as we have one belonging to my dear daughter Alice (now in Philadelphia), which would, I should  
think, fit you very well. Then, as to sitting here or there, or amusing yourself in any manner indicated, that need cause you no inconvenience. As regards your hair, it is no doubt a pity, especially as I could not help remarking its beauty during our short interview, but I am afraid that I must remain firm upon this point, and I only hope that the increased salary may recompense you for the loss. Your duties, as far as the child is concerned, are very light. Now do try to come, and I shall meet you with the dog-cart at Winchester. Let me know your train.  
-''Yours faithfully, ' 'J ephro R ucastle .'
name: Chunk 2/8

raw_entities.name: Miss Hunter
raw_entities.description: A destitute governess seeking employment, whose practical needs and personal pride are tested by an unusually lucrative but deeply eccentric job offer. She serves as the narrative voice, recounting her interview and subsequent internal conflict to Mr. Holmes.
context: You may imagine, Mr. Holmes, that to me, destitute as I was, such an offer seemed almost too good to be true.
I could hardly believe my ears. As you may observe, Mr. Holmes, my hair is somewhat luxuriant, and of a rather peculiar tint of chestnut.
I began to ask myself whether I had not done a very foolish thing.

raw_entities.name: Jephro Rucastle
raw_entities.description: A wealthy, enthusiastic employer who offers a high salary for a governess position but insists on bizarre conditions, including cutting the applicant's hair and wearing specific colors indoors. His genial demeanor masks an underlying sense of unnatural control.
context: He seemed quite enthusiastic and rubbed his hands together in the most genial fashion.
Your salary with me, madam, would commence at £ 100 a year.
Yours faithfully, ' 'J ephro R ucastle .

raw_entities.name: Miss Stoper
raw_entities.description: The sharp, pragmatic manageress of the employment agency who handles the interview and is visibly annoyed by Miss Hunter's refusal of the lucrative offer. She represents the bureaucratic side of the placement process.
context: The manageress had sat all this while busy with her papers without a word to either of us...
Well, really, it seems rather useless, since you refuse the most excellent offers in this fashion,
said she sharply.
She struck a gong upon the table, and I was shown out by the page.

raw_entities.name: Colonel Spence Munro
raw_entities.description: The previous employer of Miss Hunter, mentioned only as the source of her prior salary. His name establishes the baseline of Miss Hunter's professional experience and financial desperation.
context: I had £ 4 a month in my last place with Colonel Spence Munro.

raw_entities.name: Mrs. Rucastle
raw_entities.description: The unseen wife of Jephro Rucastle, whose peculiar tastes and demands drive the unusual conditions of the governess position. Her influence is felt through her husband's insistence on obedience to her whims.
context: Your duty would be, as I am sure your good sense would suggest, to obey any little commands my wife might give...
My wife is very anxious that you should come, for she has been much attracted by my description of you.
It is a little fancy of my wife's, and ladies' fancies, you know, madam, ladies' fancies must be consulted.

raw_entities.name: Alice
raw_entities.description: The daughter of Jephro Rucastle, currently residing in Philadelphia. She is referenced as the owner of the electric blue dress offered to Miss Hunter, hinting at a history of similar arrangements.
context: You need not, however, go to the expense of purchasing one, as we have one belonging to my dear daughter Alice (now in Philadelphia)...

raw_entities.name: The Copper Beeches
raw_entities.description: A charming country estate near Winchester where the Rucastle family resides. It serves as the proposed workplace and the focal point of the narrative's growing mystery and tension.
context: Hampshire. Charming rural place. The Copper Beeches, five miles on the far side of Winchester.
The Copper Beeches, near Winchester.

raw_entities.name: Winchester
raw_entities.description: A city near the Copper Beeches estate, serving as the designated meeting point for the governess's arrival. It acts as a transitional space between Miss Hunter's lodgings and the isolated estate.
context: The Copper Beeches, five miles on the far side of Winchester.
I shall meet you with the dog-cart at Winchester.

raw_entities.name: Philadelphia
raw_entities.description: A distant city where Alice Rucastle currently resides. Its mention underscores the geographical spread of the family and the origin of the peculiar dress offered to Miss Hunter.
context: now in Philadelphia

raw_entities.name: The Letter
raw_entities.description: A follow-up letter from Jephro Rucastle offering an increased salary and reiterating the strange conditions to persuade Miss Hunter to accept the position. It serves as the catalyst for her renewed consideration of the job.
context: I had almost overcome my pride so far as to go back to the agency and inquire whether the place was still open when I received this letter from the gentleman himself.
I have it here and I will read it to you:

raw_entities.name: The Electric Blue Dress
aliases: one belonging to my dear daughter
raw_entities.description: A specific garment requested by Mrs. Rucastle for Miss Hunter to wear indoors in the morning. It represents the eccentric demands of the employers and hints at a pattern of control over the governess's appearance.
context: My wife is fond of a particular shade of electric blue and would like you to wear such a dress indoors in the morning.
You need not, however, go to the expense of purchasing one, as we have one belonging to my dear daughter Alice...

raw_entities.name: The Pocketbook
raw_entities.description: A personal item used by Jephro Rucastle to produce a bank note as an advance on the salary. Its use underscores the immediacy and legitimacy of the financial offer, masking the underlying strangeness of the transaction.
context: The gentleman, however, seeing perhaps the look of incredulity upon my face, opened a pocketbook and took out a note.

raw_entities.name: The Governess Interview
raw_entities.description: A tense and unusual job interview where Miss Hunter is offered a lucrative position but faces bizarre demands regarding her appearance and obedience. The event establishes the central conflict and the unsettling nature of the employers.
context: You may imagine, Mr. Holmes, that to me, destitute as I was, such an offer seemed almost too good to be true.
I could hardly believe my ears. As you may observe, Mr. Holmes, my hair is somewhat luxuriant, and of a rather peculiar tint of chestnut.
He had been watching me eagerly out of his small eyes, and I could see a shadow pass over his face as I spoke.

raw_entities.name: The Salary Negotiation
raw_entities.description: A discussion where the salary is raised from a standard rate to an unusually high amount, accompanied by an advance payment and strange conditions. The negotiation highlights the financial desperation of Miss Hunter and the calculated manipulation of the employers.
context: Your salary with me, madam, would commence at £ 100 a year.
We are willing to give £ 30 a quarter, or £ 120 a year, so as to recompense you for any little inconvenience which our fads may cause you.
There was something unnatural about the whole transaction which made me wish to know a little more before I quite committed yourself.

raw_entities.name: The Employment Agency
raw_entities.description: A professional bureau run by Miss Stoper that places governesses, serving as the setting for the initial interview and the source of Miss Hunter's address. It functions as the intermediary between the desperate applicant and the eccentric employers.
context: I had almost overcome my pride so far as to go back to the agency and inquire whether the place was still open...
Miss Stoper has very kindly given me your address, and I write from here to ask you whether you have reconsidered your decision.

raw_entities.name: The Hair-Cutting Demand
raw_entities.description: An essential but unexplained requirement imposed by the employers, representing the underlying mystery and control exerted over the governess. It serves as a symbolic barrier to Miss Hunter's acceptance of the position.
context: Or to cut your hair quite short before you come to us?
It is a little fancy of my wife's, and ladies' fancies, you know, madam, ladies' fancies must be consulted.
I am afraid that it is quite essential,
said he.

raw_entities.name: The Strange Fads
raw_entities.description: The peculiar and unexplained behavioral requirements of the Rucastle family, which drive the narrative tension and hint at a darker underlying situation. They represent the theme of hidden control and the commodification of personal autonomy.
context: There was something unnatural about the whole transaction which made me wish to know a little more before I quite committed yourself.
They are not very exacting, after all.
We are faddy people, you know-faddy but kind-hearted.

properties.narrative_arc: refuses job due to bizarre demand, regrets decision, receives counter-offer

properties.physical_description: comfortable-looking, fat hands, small shining eyes, white creases on face
motivations: secure a governess who obeys his wife's whims
compensate for eccentric demands with high pay

properties.narrative_function: establishes Miss Hunter's previous low salary (£4/month)

motivations: wants governess to wear electric blue dress
wants governess to cut her hair

setting for the governess position, source of the employer's strange demands
reference point for location, meeting point for the dog-cart

properties.narrative_function: distant city where Alice is located, explains dress availability

properties.narrative_role: plot device offering increased salary and reiterating demands
properties.significance: resolves immediate rejection, pushes plot forward with counter-offer

properties.narrative_role: practical requirement for the job, symbol of the employer's fads
properties.symbolic_meaning: eccentricity and control over the governess

properties.narrative_role: demonstrates financial capability and advances salary
properties.significance: establishes credibility of the employment offer

properties.what_changed: Miss Hunter refuses the position due to the hair-cutting demand
properties.cause: conflict between Hunter's pride/principles and Rucastle's unusual demands
properties.immediate_consequence: Hunter leaves agency and begins to regret her decision
properties.why_it_matters: establishes central conflict and introduces the story's bizarre fads

properties.what_changed: Salary increased from £100 to £120 per year to compensate for fads
properties.cause: Hunter's refusal based on the hair-cutting demand
properties.immediate_consequence: Hunter receives letter offering more money and reconsiders
properties.why_it_matters: highlights transactional nature of demands and drives plot forward

properties.structure: manageress-led, uses books/records, employs a page
properties.purpose: find employment for young ladies and governesses
properties.plot_impact: facilitates meeting, highlights Hunter's destitution and agency's profit motive

properties.what_the_puzzle_is: Why must the governess cut her hair?
properties.narrative_function: central bizarre requirement driving the plot's mystery

properties.what_the_puzzle_is: The nature and purpose of the employer's eccentric demands
properties.narrative_function: establishes tone of eccentricity and control, creates central mystery
